item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons, and the second image cannot be found under any source as cc-compliant. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: author approved of edits via pm, timer removed

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This is actually pretty similar to SCP-553. Both are butterflies made up of some metal. I propose linking them.

Editing this:

SCP-1641 are unusual in their almost entirely metallic composition:

To this:

SCP-1641 are unusual in their almost entirely metallic composition:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 3 years later and the interview was never fixed! Upvoted nonetheless, but that thing seriously detracts from the general quality of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was thinking lemon in terms of being bad.

item.title: Halp!
item.content:encoded:
As this is approaching +80, I'd like to see if someone could inbox/post feedback here/hit me up in chat to help me improve the interview log.

My thoughts on it were "This guy is convinced that he's completely innocent or doing the right thing based on his religious beliefs, which generally put humanity on the bottom of the totem pole in comparison to insect, animal, and plant life. He feels totally justified in his actions, and he's also a weird, outdated hippie who speaks in antiquated terms not unlike some members of certain branches of Christianity break out Ye Olde English when discussing parts of their KJV scripture."

Yes, this is connected to 1910, and the Father he speaks of that taught him how to mod the butterflies/sent him on this mission is the same guy (or thing) that made that item and sent the other group on their mission to the logging site.

Ortiz is a South America-positioned member of an MTF that I haven't been able to fit into an article or tale yet with more presence than this nicknamed the "God Squad" that steps in on religious skips and flushes out cults. More or less an SCPF version of Ordo Malleus, except with a strict "godzilla threshold" on their anomaly usage. Otherwise, he's Standard Foundation Agent #∞. Should I give him more personality? How can I clean up this dialogue?

And of course, is the ending with them mindwiping him and handing him over to the CR gov't as a bog-standard ecoterrorist realistically what the foundation would do in this situation?

I could use some advice on making some of these points clearer, less cheesy, and more natural sounding. I'll also accept other critique on the rest of it, as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …really? I was the only one to get that on the first try?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this concept and I'm glad to see the final(er) product getting love too. Nice job Vincent_Redgrave!

Now Addendum 1641-1: the interview seems like it could be trimmed down a bit but overall I can dig it man. I do feel it works better than the voice mail before it. Recovery log is still a little flat for me but you have to remember that I came here for the exploding butterflies and that's the hook that I am most interested in. The fact that these things would be a natural plague wiping out both metal and biological components is interesting and starting to grow on me. Like the interview said, just from a swarm or two of these there was a lot of damage, and there would be much more if these things just free roamed the continent like Monarch Butterflies.

Congrats on making a cool addition to the butterfly themed SCPs that doesn't just invert butterflies and make them eat people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was also my first thought. Sparks ain't flying in /that/ way in this story lol

E:… Well, maybe for the butterflies. But I ain't travellin down that path :|

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for explaining… I was assuming they meant "lemon" as in the fanfiction term indicating an adult story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… I can see that.

By which I mean "holy fuck that's perfect why did I not know that this exists omfgyesthisisawesome"

You just influenced a skipper on the headcanon of two of his own articles.

I hope you're proud of yourself, Wilson. XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It makes me think scp-1421, personally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, why didnt anyone ask him which god he means before exchanging his memories?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nope, but I can understand how you got that idea. This is a whole other animal. What I had in my head was more or less, an ancient nature deity/something powerful enough to convince others that that's what it is, influencing a ton of nature activists into dabbling in sorcery and other dark arts like that to re-balance the conflict between nature and humanity's nature-destructive tendencies and machines. This group may or may not be responsible for my SCP-1910, as well, but I'll leave that up to reader interpretation.1

TL;DR version: Not the Broken One, but kind of his antithesis.

Footnotes
1. My headcanon says "yes", though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reading the interview, I got the idea that this guy was a CotBG cultist. I mean, using metallic creatures to destroy organic matter? Seems like something The Broken One would be very pleased with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not bad. +1

I'll jump on the "interview feels kinda off" bandwagon, but a few tweaks here and there would probably help. Maybe have the interviewer pressing for more information a little more strictly. And Richard is just a little too relaxed for an eco-terrorist who just got caught and is unable to serve his god anymore. This is an interrogation, not a talk show.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it! +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed the article, but you may not need the "creator" and his interview at all. I find it much more interesting personally if they have no constructed origin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. My personal headcanon has Srqnabotf behind this for… some reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad that my idea helped spark this cool article. Upvotes for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, no, don't apologize. You're just reinforcing what others have said, and pointed out a way I could fix it lol, which is cool for me. I'm going to try to work on a better way to handle the interview over the next couple days.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suck at writing dialogue in a clinical format… I can't even begin to describe how terrible I am at it. Have any suggestions for improvement?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the idea. Upvoted.
The interview was nice, but it doesn't sound like he's under any duress, which I think needs some editing. Don't get me wrong, the man sounds fairly natural, but it sounds like it's a casual conversation as opposed to an interview interrogation.
EDIT: Oops. About five people already said what I did. Sorry for dogpiling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like how Richard Blackbox was handled at the end. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A fun concept, and I liked the interview. I almost typed "internet". I still like the internet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Richard: Well, I ain’t gonna give you any runaround, Cap.

mfw steve mcqueen is a new person of interest

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I said in the chat, I like this version, and I think it's better with the interview than without in. However, I'm going to have to agree that the creator's dialogue in the interview feels a bit wooden and unnatural, and he just doesn't feel like a believable character. Still, it got a solid +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After re-reading it, I've realized that I really dislike the interview log the most. The dialogue just doesn't feel natural; all the "man"s and "ain't"s just came across as a weak attempt to make him sound like a real character.

This is very much the case. In both behavior and speech, it doesn't sound like a real person, it sounds like someone trying to sound like a real person.
Overly relaxed and familiar tone, forced "natural" speech mannerisms and slang…pretty much the standard Interviewed. I'm surprised the interviewer wasn't a doctor, so that he could be repeatedly referred to as "doc".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do you know who I am!? I AM THE MAN WHO'S GONNA BURN YOUR HOUSE DOWN! WITH THE LEMONS! I'M GONNA GET MY ENGINEERS TO INVENT A COMBUSTIBLE LEMON, THAT BURN YOUR HOUSE DOWN!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Lemon version"?

And yeah, I went through about six different versions of the interview log before I got to this point, and was comfortable with the information revealed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was all set to upvote until I read the interview log. That kinda killed it for me. I'm tired of anything involving people's respective god(s), and the interview didn't really make me think about much, except for the fact that apparently some random guy was able to create combustible butterflies. Someone get to work on a lemon version of this ASAP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, I liked it. Upvoted.

Now for the criticism:

I didn't really like the backstory. It felt extremely generic. "Some random guy does some weird mysterious ritual to summon them for an unexplained reason". I'll give you credit for partially averting the trope by including the fact that A) he didn't commit suicide after and B) motives are hinted at, but that wasn't enough to make me like it. Shouldn't the Foundation have leaned on him a little more, to understand how he pulled it off?

EDIT/UPDATE: After re-reading it, I've realized that I really dislike the interview log the most. The dialogue just doesn't feel natural; all the "man"s and "ain't"s just came across as a weak attempt to make him sound like a real character.Combine that with an uninspired story it's just all-around mediocre.

Still upvoted. It isn't that that it completely ruins, it's just that the SCP works for me in spite of the log/backstory, instead of because of it.

Also, I actually laughed out loud at

each individual breeding pair is to be placed inside a separate blast chamber

Just the entire imagery of their mating is fantastic. Reminds me of the scene from Futurama where Lur, Ruler of Omicron Persei 8 is about to mate with his wife.

EDIT #2: I just realized another (somewhat fixable) issue I have with the interview log is: Richard is way to relaxed and confident. I mean, even if you are capable of creating these things, shouldn't he be a bit unnerved that he was caught? Doesn't the fact that the Foundation is acting extremely relaxed and confident make him a bit uneasy? I mean, he literally created an impossible creature, and here this Commander Ortiz guy is talking about it like it isn't a big deal.

Interviews are scary, intimidating. Ever talk to the police? Even if you haven't done anything wrong, it's nervewracking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I still think the Ecoterrorist sounds a little flat for some reason, but I love the concept now as much as I did when I first read it in Brainstorming.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Special thanks to Roget, Aelanna, Photosynthetic, Meserach, Anborough, Gaffney, and the dozens of others who reviewed and helped me rewrite this until it was satisfactorily done, to me at least. Hope you enjoy it.
